speaker: interviewee
text: [AUDIO OUT]

speaker: interviewee
text: --spelt in English, my last name is R-A-N-D. And my first name is Y-A-A-C-O-V.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Hebrew and English name or any other language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only on one occasion, while coming out from Cyprus illegally to Palestine at that time, I came out under the name of [? Nathan ?] [? Nathanson, ?] aged 17. And I still have the passport of this-- of this traveling to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The official date of birth is 26 of November, 1926. But I was really born November the 1st the same year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was there a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because we lived in a kind of a village. And-- and my father registered me only in January, 1927. And I wanted to correct this and I came to Israel and I made a mistake. But so it stays in my-- all of my documents.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In November, I'll be 70, 7-0.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the little village in Bukovina, in southern Bukovina, actually in Romania, called Arbore, A-R-B-O-R-E. And it is in the county of Suceave, S-U-C-E-A-V-E, which was quite a Jewish town before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: First perhaps may I tell you a little bit about the little village? That was-- I call it the little village because the village makes it little, but in essence, it was a village having about 10,000 habitants and about 30 Jewish families. There was a very big population of German origin, who immigrated to Germany when Hitler took out all the Volksdeutsche. And there I was born. Most of the families were religious. There were only two or three families which were not religious. There was a synagogue and a little cheder. And we studied in that cheder. I start to study at the age of 2 and 1/2 to read. And at the age of 3 and 1/2 I studied also in the winter, dark nights, walking over to the cheder with a little lamp in my hand. We didn't-- at that time, we didn't have fear to walk in the night, even if it was in a goyish village.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the town look like, this village? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: This village, there were little houses. The Jewish houses were mostly in the center of the village. Almost all of them were businessmen or small businessmen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to sell food, to sell basic clothing, to sell little wheels or all that which is needed in an agricultural area, most of them. Nothing special in this respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your house look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I remember this house, because I left around the age of five or six years, it was a new house, probably built by my father. I mean, not himself, but was built for my father. My father had a little shop there in the house. Around the house--

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of shop was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- as I told you he had also a monopoly on cigarettes, which at that time was a monopoly of the government. And he had the right to sell cigarettes. Around the house there were a little ground. My late mother, [NON-ENGLISH], she used to grow there some basic vegetables like onion, garlic, et cetera, et cetera, and some flowers around the house. And we loved very much to go just to this little field and take out some good radish, fresh radish, and to have breakfast with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In terms of space? I couldn't tell you, but it was big.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Come on. You're joking. Nobody had running water. I just can tell you that 51 years later after I left this little village and I came back to the village and stood at the well where my grandfather-- in the-- in the courtyard of my grandfather we used to take this wheel to take out water from that well. I was so moved. You can't imagine. But running water, nobody knew about that. The same, there were no toilets in the houses. Toilets were always outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no address. You just mentioned the village. And the postman, he knew all the people in that village. No address.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to tell us about your family. Could you start with your father, his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, as I said at the beginning, his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. But at that time, he was called Leibisch, L-E-I-B-I-C-- S-C-H. He was-- until 1932, he was a kommersant, as I described before. In 1932-- and if I am wrong the months or-- I am not so exact with these kinds of historical dates-- the-- the Romanian government made a law called the [NON-ENGLISH], which means the converting law. According to this law, all the non-Jewish people who owed a lot of money from merchandise that they took from my father, they were exempted from paying their debts, so my father became quite, I would say, a poor man. And he decided to use his vast knowledge in Judaic studies. And he went to Suceava, to one of the greatest rabbinical figures of those times in Romania called Rabbi Meshulam Rath, R-A-T-H. He was at that time chief rabbi in Suceava. And he studied with him [NON-ENGLISH], which is slaughtering, ritual slaughtering; [NON-ENGLISH], which is to be a mohel, and he had also a wonderful voice and knew a lot of how to pray Jewish prayers, how liturgical prayers in-- in-- in Jewish holidays and Shabbat and so on, so he had something in hand to make a living. And then we left this village and we went to a small city in Bessarabia called Sculeni, S-C-U-L-E-N-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he found there a position, simply. But he was not happy there, because there was no-- there were very few people religious in that community. And for my father, the education of his children, that was the most important thing. And he felt that we cannot get the appropriate education there. So about a year later, we left this community. By the way, in Borough Park, there is Rabbi Yisroel from Sculeni, the Sculeni rabbi. And we studied together in cheder in Sculeni. I just visit him a few years ago. He was very astonished to see me there, but he remembered me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel seeing him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He remembered my father. I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very moved, because, you know, we were only three children in that cheder, the rabbi, my late brother, and myself. And the teachers were his father and my father. That was the cheder. So you know, it was very moving to see him again. From there we went into a place in Moldova called Bracesdi, B-R-A-C-E-S-D-I, which was a bigger community. And my father had there a nicer salary. And there he had also a synagogue to be hazzan, which he did not have in Sculeni. And there we stayed for about two years and something. And again for the same problem, my father was born in the Bukovina, he wanted to go back to Bukovina. And so we ended up finally in a little town in the Bukovina, which is a beautiful place, marvelous sceneries there, called Vatra-Dornei, V-A-T-R-A dash D-O-R-N-E-I, called-- under the Austria-Hungarian rule, called Dorna-Watra, D-O-R-N-A-W-A-T-R-A. And there I've been until we were deported to the concentration camps. And that is where I spent my beautiful years of-- as a youngster, as a child and a youngster.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back now. Could you tell me about your mother, her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in another village in the neighborhood of Arbore called Balaceala, B-A-L-A-C-E-A-L-A, also county of Suceave, to a very-- to parents who were [NON-ENGLISH] Hasidim. My father, by the way, was [NON-ENGLISH] Hasidim. They were a little bit of competitive to each other. And there was a very interesting thing that a [NON-ENGLISH] married a [NON-ENGLISH] from the house of [NON-ENGLISH] Hasidim. He was a very honest, pious man, my grandfather. But to come back to my mother I'll perhaps say something about my grandparents also. And they married very young. They-- it was an interesting thing. The father of the bride, of my mother, sent a commission, a committee to the house of my father's parents to see if he is a [NON-ENGLISH], if he knows something, who this guy was-- is. And they found him probably adequate, because the result was they ended and they-- and they married. And she-- most of her years, she was a housewife, until later on when we came to Israel. And that's already a story for later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us a full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's full name is Iuta Feigi, I-U-T-A another name F-E-I-G-I. Family name was Hass, H-A- double S. The-- her ancestors-- I don't know exactly to which generation to go back-- were the founders of that community in this village.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back to your mother, what thought comes to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What thought, what memory of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, my mother was the most loyal human being that you can imagine to her family, to her children. And just to illustrate this, my father's father in his latest years of life lived in our-- in our house. And he had some special condition to be treated. And she-- it was not always pleasant to do that. She did it until the last of his days, to the extent that for years my father and my mother could never go out somewhere together. Always one had to stay with my grandfather. She did it full-heartedly. I never heard any complaint about this. And she was just a very good human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: So because I'm talking now about my mother, I'll start from the grandparents of my mother. Excuse me, please. [DRINKS] As most of the Jews in that village and the villages around, he had also a shop, where he was selling all kinds of things, like wheat and sugar and flour and all kind of these things to the people living in that village. But he had also some fields outside of the village where I like to go with one of his daughters who used to drive the horses there. And we used to collect the-- the what has grown on the fields and to come back on a high wagon full with all this stuff. And they had also cows in-- in the courtyard, so we had fresh milk. My grandmother used to-- to make butter out of the milk. And she was a very good cook, so all these kinds are together in my picture from this little village. I also remember the evening of Shabbat in that village. All of the people in that village gathered in the house of my grandfather. And they used to tell Hasidic stories. They used to sing together. My grandmother, I see her before my eyes standing and watching with such a huge pleasure what was going on in her house. That's the kind of a house. They were-- were honest people, good Jews, and good human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names. The name of my grandfather was Yehoshua [NON-ENGLISH] Hass. And the name of my grandmother was Baila, but I do not remember what was her last name, her maiden name. And I don't remember the name of the parents. I never met them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now about your other grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my father's parents, the name of my father-grandfather was Shalom Yosef. That's also the name my son has, because he was born a year my grandfather passed away. And he was a-- as we call it a [NON-ENGLISH]. He lived in [INAUDIBLE]. He was a personal friend with the rabbi from [INAUDIBLE]. They were very, very close to each other. And my grandmother, her name was Esther Sarah. She-- her maiden name was Eisenthal, E-I-S-E-N-T-H-A-L. The daughter of Chaim and I think Miriam Eisenthal. I am not quite sure about that. And they-- my grandfather left [NON-ENGLISH] because he married this girl. And then they started their life in that place called Arbore. And they were there even later than our family, because he still had the means to live there until-- as all the Jews from the-- from the different villages were chased into the cities. And so he came also to Vatra-Dornei, where he came with his other two sons-- with two of his sons. And so we were together in this little place. And we were deported together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not have any brother alive now. I had one brother called Menashe, [NON-ENGLISH], M-E-N-A-S-H-E. He passed away at the age of 11 and 1/2 on a heart condition in Arbore and is buried there. A few years ago I was there and his gravestone is still there, fully readable, and I have pictures of that. I could say kaddish there, despite the fact [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't have any minyan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the next child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the next child--

speaker: interviewee
text: Next child--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: --that's me. And then I have a sister called Chana. She is born 1932. She lives actually in Israel, in Hadera. Married, has three children and grandchildren. Baruch Hashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her last name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gur. Chana Gur. Her husband is [NON-ENGLISH] Gur. And then I have two twin sisters. One is Miriam, married to Baruch Friedman, F-R-I-E-D-M-A-N. And they live in Petah Tikva, Israel. They have also four sons and grandchildren. Baruch Hashem. And then the-- the missing kid, that is Rachel. She is born two hours later than Miriam, about two hours. And she's married to [INAUDIBLE] Rabinowitz, R-A-B-I-N-O-W-I-T-Z. And they live in Immanuel, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your daily life like growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daily life in the little village, I have to make-- to separate my little village from what went then all in Vatra-Donrei. But in the little village, I used to go to the cheder. That was all the day. We used to play very simple play-- games like hiding-- like hiding on trees. And when I came, as I told you, 51 years later to that village and I found the tree that we used to play around in the garden of my grandfather at a little river which is going there, a lindenbaum, I can tell you I cried. We used also to play with a wheel that we used with a little stick to run and to see who can run farther. We had our little cheder games, like to make little squares, who ends the square, et cetera. And Shabbat, it was-- in addition to what is a regular Shabbat [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] program, we used to go-- I used to go to my grand-grandfather, Chaim Eisenthal, who was a very big erudite. And he was a [NON-ENGLISH], which was a very famous person that time in Poland. And he used to test me, to examine me if I remember what I studied in the cheder, if I know that well. And at the end, he used to give me-- he had this little shop next to his house. He could go in from the house. He went to the shop and took out a red candy. You don't know what taste has such a candy. That was the recompense when I knew well. Even if I didn't know so well, also I got the candy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbos like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos, like in any Jewish house, we kept strictly Shabbos. And usually, especially in the summertime, I-- for the [NON-ENGLISH] I went to the-- we went all to the grandfather. And he at that time, we liked very much strawberries, but not for that you buy here at the grocery stores, but strawberries that been picked up in the woods, little strawberries. This with cream cheese or with sour cream, that was a real pleasure. And we added a lot of sugar on that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Shabbos meal like, the Friday night meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like today, except one thing. We didn't know there what is cholent. Cholent was a totally different story. Cholent was the soup and the meat that has been put into a little [NON-ENGLISH], a little oven, which was never heated directly, but it got the heat from the adjacent oven. And there it came out brown, good. And I don't find that here. But that was our cholent. Not-- not as we know it, cholent with [NON-ENGLISH] and all kind of this stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the cholent? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meat and soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any specific--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the cholent.

speaker: interviewer
text: --ingredients?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they put in? At that time, it wasn't of-- [LAUGHS] on my-- my list of interests.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sing zemiros?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sing zemiros on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any?

speaker: interviewee
text: All our family was singing, first of all. My grandfather was a [NON-ENGLISH]. My father was singing. I'm a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had all very strong inclinations towards music. You would like me to sing you something from those times? OK. I sing you [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] It so goes on. I didn't finish it. I don't want to take too much of time. But I must say once thing, that we didn't sing that like I did. We used to sing it in the Ashkenazish accent, like [NON-ENGLISH SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so on. What was Pesach like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pesach we used to make together. My grandfather had at that time there four sons. One is my late father. Then there was a son also called Yakov Pinchas. He is not any more alive. And then there are three we are still alive, Shimon Moshe, Zvi, and Zev.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had five sons. We used to be all together and the grandchildren. Our pleasure was to open up the boxes with the Pesach [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] kitchen-- kitchen tools and so on. And every child that this little glass for the [INAUDIBLE]. And there is a lot of that but I feel that we have to stop now? [INAUDIBLE]
